claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**Let's start with the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child? Please let me know which category applies:

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Also, could you tell me the child's name so I can keep track during our assessment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm inquiring about my son Alex. He's 8 years old, so definitely under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Since Alex is under 16, he automatically qualifies based on age. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work status:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

At 8 years old, I expect the answer is yes (not working), but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all. He's only 8, so he's just at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since Alex isn't working, let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, this refers to benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit. At 8 years old, I wouldn't expect him to be receiving these, but I just need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

At 8 years old and in school, I'm confident the answer is no, but I need to confirm. (This check is mainly relevant for older children, as certain apprenticeships can affect eligibility.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. He's only 8 and just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly as expected! Let me move on to the next part of the assessment, which will be about your situation as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. **You only need to meet ONE of the following criteria to qualify:**

**Do any of these apply to you?**

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident living in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these best describes your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. I'm a resident here.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that satisfies the residency requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex. **You only need to meet ONE of the following criteria:**

**Do any of these apply to you?**

1. **Living with Alex** - Alex lives with you
2. **Contributing to Alex's upkeep** - You contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these describes your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that confirms your responsibility for Alex. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming (or trying to claim) Child Benefit for Alex?**

- **No** - No one else is claiming for Alex
- **Yes, but we've agreed** - Someone else was claiming, but you've both agreed that you should be the one to claim instead
- **Yes, and it's disputed** - There's a disagreement about who should claim for Alex

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **At home normally** - Living at home with you in a normal arrangement
- **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - Currently in local authority care
- **In foster care** - Currently being fostered

Which describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for a few months.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. Let me check the rules around that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I need to understand more about Alex's hospital stay.

**Important information:** Child Benefit can continue for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital. There's also a linking rule: if a child returns to hospital within **28 days** of a previous stay, the time is added together cumulatively.

**Can you tell me:**
1. Approximately how long has Alex been in hospital during this current stay?
2. Has he had any previous hospital stays in the past few months? (If so, were any of them separated by less than 28 days?)

This will help me determine whether the 12-week limit applies to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for about 15 weeks now in this stay. There haven't been any previous stays before this one.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex has been in hospital for about 15 weeks, that exceeds the 12-week limit. However, there's still a way you may remain eligible. Let me check the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - there's an exception to the 12-week rule!

**Even though Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, you may still be eligible if you are regularly spending money on him while he's there.**

This could include things like:
- Buying him clothes, toys, or books
- Bringing him food or treats
- Paying for entertainment (games, magazines, etc.)
- Any other regular spending for his care or comfort

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?**

(Note: If you have a spouse or civil partner living with you, their spending also counts.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy him clothes, magazines, and his favorite snacks when I visit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - that means the exception applies to you. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's hospital stay being funded through the NHS or your own means, rather than the local council paying for his care or accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the NHS is covering the hospital stay. The council isn't paying anything towards his care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That completes the assessment. Let me now provide you with the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary for Alex:
✅ **Age requirement** - Alex is under 16  
✅ **Work status** - Not working (as expected at age 8)  
✅ **No benefits in own right** - Alex doesn't receive ESA or Universal Credit  
✅ **Not in apprenticeship** - Still in primary school  
✅ **Your residency** - You live in the UK  
✅ **Responsibility** - Alex lives with you  
✅ **No competing claims** - No one else is claiming for Alex  
✅ **Hospital stay exception** - Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (over the 12-week limit), you qualify for the exception because you're **regularly spending money on him** during his stay  
✅ **No council funding** - The NHS is covering his care, not the local council

---

### Next Steps to Claim:
1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Be aware**: If you or a partner earn over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

👉 For more information and to apply: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

I hope Alex recovers well and is home soon. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 8) meets all Child Benefit eligibility criteria: 1) He is under 16 years old, which automatically qualifies him by age. 2) He is not working (under 24 hours per week requirement met). 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit). 4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. 5) The claimant is a UK resident. 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. 7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. 8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies for the exception because they are regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay (clothes, magazines, snacks). 9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - the NHS is covering the hospital stay.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for longer than the standard 12-week limit, the claimant meets the exception criteria by regularly spending money on Alex during his stay. All other eligibility requirements are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 8) meets all Child Benefit eligibility criteria: 1) He is under 16 years old, which automatically qualifies him by age. 2) He is not working (under 24 hours per week requirement met). 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit). 4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. 5) The claimant is a UK resident. 6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as he lives with them. 7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. 8) Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies for the exception because they are regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay (clothes, magazines, snacks). 9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - the NHS is covering the hospital stay.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for longer than the standard 12-week limit, the claimant meets the exception criteria by regularly spending money on Alex during his stay. All other eligibility requirements are satisfied.
